title: The Vineyard Let Out to Tenants
subtitle: The householder plants a vineyard, lets it to the husbandmen, and at last sends his Son

stillness: Be still, and let the vineyard rise before you.
A wall, a winepress, a tower — and a Lord who goes far away.
Sit quietly, for this vineyard is also your own soul.
gospel: There was a householder who planted a vineyard,
and set a hedge round about it,
and dug in it a winepress,
and let it out to husbandmen; and went into a far country.
And when the time of the fruits drew near, he sent his servants to the husbandmen, that they might receive the fruits of it.
And the husbandmen, having laid hands on his servants, beat one, and killed another, and stoned another.
Again he sent other servants, more than the former; and they did to them in like manner.
And last of all he sent to them his Son, saying: They will reverence my Son.
But the husbandmen seeing the Son, said: This is the heir; come, let us kill him, and we shall have his inheritance.
And taking him, they cast him forth out of the vineyard, and killed him.
Therefore I say to you, that the kingdom of God shall be taken from you, and shall be given to a nation yielding the fruits thereof.
behold: Behold the householder, who by nature is Lord, by goodwill a Father.
He took the choicest vines out of Egypt and planted them in the land of promise.
He set a hedge about his vineyard — a threefold guard: the angels over the walls of Jerusalem, the protection of fighting men, the fortress of its ramparts.
He dug in it a winepress — the law of justice, the fear that presses down and torments the wicked — and he dug it with signs, with threats, and with promises.
He built a tower — the temple for the worship of God, called a tower for the safety of its refuge.
He let it out to husbandmen — to the priests and the princes, who were to root out the vices like thorns, and graft in the virtues like good seed.
And he went into a far country — not by changing his place, for God is everywhere, but because he left the vinedressers their free will.
He who is everywhere present is more present to those who love, and more absent to those who neglect him.
When the time of the fruits drew near, he sent his servants — the prophets — that they might lead the people back to the fruit of good works.
But the husbandmen laid hands on them: one they beat, another they killed, another they stoned.
And last of all he sent his Son, made flesh for us, the heir of all things and the prince.
And they cast him out beyond the gate of Jerusalem, and killed him, crucifying him — coveting to hold his inheritance for themselves.
consider: Consider their ingratitude: to them God gave so many benefits, and they rendered murder.
They reasoned within themselves: if this people take him for their Lord, we must lose the custom of our victims and sacrifices, which is set up for our gain.
But if we kill him, the custom of offering will be held forever, and so it shall become our possession, and shall be our inheritance.
Therefore the Lord came to judge, and to avenge this deed.
He destroyed the wicked wickedly, when their city and their temple were thrown down, and their people slain and led captive.
And the kingdom of God — Christ, the Scriptures, the faith, the Church, life everlasting — was taken away from them.
Now turn the parable upon yourself: for each one of us is a vineyard, and each is also the keeper, for each one tills his own soul.
The rational soul is let out to man, poured into the body for this: that through it he may bear the fruit of good works.
And in every good of a man — good thought, good work, good word — five grapes grow.
Four remain to the man: gladness of heart, remission of guilt, escape from eternal death, the winning of eternal glory.
But the fifth grape is the glory of God: this we must give not to ourselves, but to God.
Happy are they who till well and neglect not, who increase and diminish not, who keep and destroy not.
prayer: The vineyard has been let out to us, O Lord,
and it is let out on this condition:
that we render the fruit to the Lord in his seasons,
and that we know, each one of us,
what we owe of the fruit of our vineyard.
Make us husbandmen who till well and neglect not,
and the fifth grape, the glory, we give not to ourselves, but to you.

latinJewel.latin: Qui ubique præsens est, diligentibus præsentior, et negligentibus absentior est.
latinJewel.english: He who is everywhere present is more present to those who love him, and more absent to those who neglect him.
